text.title: Help and Documentation — Examples and Antipatterns
text.summary: Concrete good and bad examples of help and documentation implementation across real products.

example.note: Notion: contextual ? icon on each feature, searchable help center, video tutorials, template gallery (learning by example). Help is accessible without leaving the product and organized by user task.

Stripe: comprehensive documentation with code examples in multiple languages, interactive API explorer, contextual tooltips in dashboard. Help is written at the developer's level of abstraction and tested against real API responses.

Slack: Slackbot answers questions in-context, keyboard shortcut guide (?/), getting started checklist for onboarding, searchable help center. Multiple help types serve different user states (first-time, returning, expert).

antipattern.description: Help buried in footer: no contextual assistance in the product, users must search the entire site to find an answer.
antipattern.reason: Users in an error state or confusion moment will not navigate away from their current task to find help; they will abandon or contact support instead.

antipattern.description: Outdated documentation: screenshots from an old version, features that no longer exist, broken links.
antipattern.reason: Stale screenshots actively mislead users; they follow steps that no longer match the UI and conclude the product is broken, not the documentation.

antipattern.description: Technical overload: documentation assumes expertise, no beginner content, jargon without explanation.
antipattern.reason: Help written for the product team's level of knowledge fails new users who lack that vocabulary; support ticket volume increases for questions that beginner documentation would answer.

section.title: Help Content Types by Phase
section.p: Onboarding phase: welcome tours (first login), empty states (new sections with action guidance), progress checklist (guide setup completion).
In-context phase: tooltips on hover (explain specific elements), inline hints always visible (guide input), ? icons on demand (detailed explanation).
Reference phase: FAQs (quick answers to common questions), how-tos (step-by-step task guidance), reference docs (complete information).
Support phase: search (self-service answer finding), chat (real-time human assistance), email (complex issue resolution).

list.item: Help searches: what users look for — reveals vocabulary gaps
Search success rate: do users find answers? — reveals content gaps
Zero-results searches: explicit content gap signal, track weekly
Support tickets: questions that help should already answer
Feature adoption rate: does contextual help increase usage?
